The depression of the mid-1890s put many out of work — soup kitchens and relief programs appeared. Downstate Illinois remained agricultural and conservative, viewing Chicago with a mix of awe and suspicion.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Panic of 1893 causes mass unemployment and suffering
Pullman Strike — federal troops crush workers' rebellion (1894)
Industrial accidents and unsafe working conditions continue
Overcrowded immigrant neighborhoods with poor sanitation
H.H. Holmes's murder castle discovered near the Exposition grounds
narrative_hooks: A visitor walks through the White City of the Columbian Exposition in 1893, marveling at electric lights and grand architecture — then steps outside the fairgrounds into neighborhoods of poverty and despair
A Pullman worker watches federal troops march down his street in 1894, breaking the strike that was his only weapon against a company that controls his wages, his rent, and his life
Ida B. Wells arrives in Chicago carrying the records of lynchings she has documented across the South, determined to make the nation listen
A Lithuanian butcher in the Back of the Yards works on the killing floor, his hands raw, processing animals whose final journey Upton Sinclair will one day describe

key_events: Upton Sinclair's 'The Jungle' exposes meatpacking industry conditions, leading to the Pure Food and Drug Act (1906)
Chicago's population reaches 2.2 million by 1910 — the nation's railroad, meatpacking, and manufacturing capital
Iroquois Theatre fire kills 602 people — the deadliest single-building fire in American history (December 30, 1903)
Chicago's South Side becomes a destination for early Black migrants from the South
Illinois coal mine disasters: Cherry Mine fire kills 259 miners (November 13, 1909)
Progressive reform movement strengthens — factory inspections, child labor laws, and workers' compensation
1900s.economic_conditions: Chicago's economy was one of the largest and most diversified in the world. Meatpacking, steel, railroads, grain, lumber, and manufacturing generated enormous wealth. Upton Sinclair's expose of the stockyards did not reduce production but forced sanitary reforms. The Pure Food and Drug Act (1906) was a direct result. Southern Illinois's coal mining industry employed tens of thousands. Agriculture remained the economic foundation of downstate. Chicago's financial institutions — banks, insurance companies, and the Board of Trade — served the entire Midwest.
1900s.migration_patterns: Eastern and Southern European immigration reached its peak. Chicago's ethnic neighborhoods expanded — Polish communities in the Northwest Side, Italian neighborhoods in Little Italy and the Near West Side, Jewish communities in Maxwell Street, Lithuanian and Czech neighborhoods on the South and West Sides. Black migration from the South increased, establishing the foundations of the Black Belt on Chicago's South Side. Rural-to-urban migration continued from downstate and neighboring states.
1900s.social_context: The Progressive Era brought reform to Illinois. Jane Addams was a national figure. Factory inspectors documented dangerous conditions. Child labor laws were passed. Workers' compensation was debated. The Cherry Mine disaster of 1909 — 259 miners killed — shocked the state and nation. Upton Sinclair's 'The Jungle' (1906) intended to expose the exploitation of workers but instead triggered food safety reform. Chicago's ethnic neighborhoods created rich cultural ecosystems but also competition for jobs and political power. Racial tensions began building as the South Side Black Belt grew.
1900s.daily_life_snapshot: Chicago in the 1900s was a city of smokestacks, stockyards, and streetcars. Workers commuted on the 'L' and crowded streetcars to factories, offices, and shops. Ethnic neighborhoods were vibrant but often overcrowded — extended families shared small apartments. Department stores, nickelodeons, and amusement parks provided entertainment. Public schools served immigrant children who learned English while their parents maintained Old World languages at home. Downstate Illinois was a world of farms, small towns, and coal-mining communities — connected to Chicago by rail but culturally distant.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Iroquois Theatre fire kills 602 people in Chicago (December 30, 1903)
Cherry Mine disaster kills 259 miners in central Illinois (November 13, 1909)
Unsafe working conditions in stockyards and factories exposed by Sinclair
Child labor in factories and mines
Overcrowded immigrant housing and poor sanitation
narrative_hooks: Upton Sinclair walks through the Back of the Yards in 1904, notebook in hand, documenting the horrors that will become 'The Jungle'
A mother at the Iroquois Theatre tries to reach her children as fire engulfs the building on December 30, 1903 — 602 people will die, many of them women and children at a holiday matinee
A miner trapped in the Cherry Mine in November 1909 scratches a message to his family on a piece of timber, not knowing if he will survive
A Polish girl on the Northwest Side of Chicago learns English at a public school and translates for her parents at the grocery store — two worlds in one family

key_events: Great Migration transforms Chicago's South Side — Black population grows from 44,000 (1910) to 109,000 (1920)
Chicago Defender newspaper, founded by Robert Abbott, actively recruits Black Southerners to Chicago
United States enters World War I (April 1917); Illinois sends over 350,000 troops
Race riot in East St. Louis kills an estimated 39-200 Black residents (July 2, 1917)
Chicago Race Riot: five days of racial violence leave 38 dead and 500 injured (July 27–August 3, 1919)
Eighteenth Amendment (Prohibition) ratified — Illinois becomes a battleground for bootlegging
Women gain full suffrage in Illinois for presidential elections (1913)
1910s.economic_conditions: World War I supercharged Illinois's industrial economy. Chicago's factories, stockyards, and steel mills ran at full capacity producing war material. The labor shortage created by the war and immigration restrictions opened factory jobs to Black migrants from the South for the first time on a large scale. Wages rose but inflation eroded gains. Agriculture boomed as European demand for American food increased. After the war, the economy initially boomed before adjusting to peacetime production. The wartime labor migration permanently changed Chicago's demographics.
1910s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration was the transformative event of the decade for Illinois. The Chicago Defender, the nation's most influential Black newspaper, actively recruited Southern Blacks with stories of opportunity and freedom in Chicago. Tens of thousands answered the call. The Black Belt on the South Side expanded rapidly. Appalachian whites also migrated to Chicago and downstate industrial cities. European immigration slowed during the war. Anti-German sentiment affected Illinois's large German-American population. The East St. Louis and Chicago race riots reflected white resistance to Black migration.
1910s.social_context: The Great Migration created a Black metropolis on Chicago's South Side that would become the cultural capital of Black America. But white resistance was fierce and violent — the East St. Louis race riot of 1917 and the Chicago race riot of 1919 were among the worst racial violence in American history. The 1919 riot was triggered by the murder of a Black teenager swimming in Lake Michigan who drifted into the 'white' section. Five days of violence devastated the Black Belt and neighboring Irish neighborhoods. The labor movement grew during the war but faced backlash afterward. Prohibition was about to transform Chicago's underworld.
1910s.daily_life_snapshot: Wartime Chicago pulsed with energy and tension. Factories ran around the clock. New arrivals from the South crowded into the Black Belt's kitchenette apartments — single rooms with shared bathrooms. Black-owned businesses, churches, and cultural institutions flourished on South State Street. Jazz and blues migrated north with the people. White ethnic neighborhoods guarded their boundaries. Victory gardens, war bond drives, and influenza anxiety marked daily life. The 1918 flu pandemic killed thousands in Chicago. After the race riot of 1919, Black and white Chicagoans lived in an uneasy, enforced separation.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: East St. Louis race riot — estimated 39-200 Black people killed (July 2, 1917)
Chicago Race Riot — 38 dead, 500 injured, 1,000 Black families homeless (July 1919)
1918 influenza pandemic kills thousands in Illinois
World War I casualties — thousands of Illinoisans killed and wounded
Anti-German sentiment targets Illinois's German-American communities
narrative_hooks: A Black family from Mississippi reads the Chicago Defender on a borrowed newspaper and decides to take the Illinois Central north to the promised land of the South Side

Eugene Williams, a seventeen-year-old Black teenager, floats on a raft in Lake Michigan on July 27, 1919 — he drifts past an invisible line, and a white man throws a rock that will ignite five days of war
A woman in East St. Louis watches her neighborhood burn in July 1917, driven from her home by white mobs for the crime of being Black in a city that needed her labor
A German-American butcher on Chicago's North Side anglicizes his name during the war, dropping the umlauts that connected him to the old country

key_events: Prohibition transforms Chicago into the bootlegging capital of America — Al Capone rises to power
St. Valentine's Day Massacre: Capone's men gun down seven rivals (February 14, 1929)
Chicago's Black Belt becomes Bronzeville — a vibrant center of Black culture, business, and politics
Great Migration continues — Chicago's Black population reaches 234,000 by 1930
Chicago's jazz and blues scene flourishes — Louis Armstrong, King Oliver, and others transform American music
William 'Big Bill' Thompson serves as Republican mayor, courting Black votes and tolerating organized crime
Immigration restriction acts (1921, 1924) reduce European immigration
1920s.economic_conditions: The Roaring Twenties brought prosperity and notoriety to Chicago. Manufacturing, meatpacking, and commerce thrived. Prohibition created an enormous illegal economy — bootlegging, speakeasies, and gambling generated millions for organized crime. Legitimate businesses also boomed — department stores, construction, and the emerging advertising industry. Bronzeville's Black economy flourished with banks, insurance companies, newspapers, and entertainment venues. Agriculture downstate struggled as commodity prices remained depressed. The stock market speculative bubble grew dangerous.
1920s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration continued at high volume — Chicago's Black population nearly tripled during the 1920s. Bronzeville on the South Side became the nation's largest and most culturally significant Black community. Immigration restriction acts reduced but did not eliminate European immigration. Mexican migration to Chicago began in significant numbers for the first time, drawn by railroad and meatpacking jobs. Rural-to-urban migration continued. Chicago's population approached 3.4 million by 1930. Ethnic succession transformed neighborhoods — as established groups moved out, new arrivals moved in.
1920s.social_context: Chicago in the 1920s was simultaneously the most culturally creative and the most criminally corrupt city in America. Bronzeville was the cultural capital of Black America — jazz and blues clubs, the Chicago Defender, Black-owned businesses, and intellectual life flourished. But housing segregation was brutally enforced through restrictive covenants, bombings, and violence. Al Capone's criminal empire controlled much of the city's illegal economy with the complicity of corrupt politicians and police. Mayor Thompson's machine politics traded tolerance for Black votes. The labor movement struggled against open-shop campaigns. Downstate Illinois remained agricultural and conservative.
1920s.daily_life_snapshot: Chicago in the 1920s was a city of jazz, bootleg whiskey, skyscrapers, and stockyards. Bronzeville's nightclubs drew audiences from across the city and the nation. Speakeasies operated openly in many neighborhoods. The 'L' trains connected a sprawling metropolis. Ethnic neighborhoods maintained distinct identities — Italian Taylor Street, Polish Milwaukee Avenue, Jewish Lawndale. Department stores on State Street defined consumer culture. Working-class families worked in factories and meatpacking plants. Radio brought new entertainment home. Downstate, farm families struggled with low prices while small-town life continued its familiar rhythms.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Organized crime violence — St. Valentine's Day Massacre and hundreds of other killings
Racial violence and bombings against Black families moving into white neighborhoods
Housing segregation confines Black Chicagoans to overcrowded South Side
Agricultural depression hits downstate farmers throughout the decade
Corruption pervades city government and police
narrative_hooks: Louis Armstrong blows his trumpet on the South Side in the 1920s, and the sound that comes out changes American music forever
A Black family on the South Side finds a bomb on their porch — the price of trying to move one block past the invisible color line
Al Capone sits in his suite at the Lexington Hotel, running a criminal empire that earns more than many legitimate corporations
A Mexican family arrives in Chicago's South Chicago neighborhood to work in the steel mills, adding another thread to the city's ethnic tapestry

key_events: Great Depression devastates Chicago and Illinois — unemployment reaches 50% in some neighborhoods
Century of Progress International Exposition in Chicago (1933–1934) — a Depression-era world's fair
Al Capone convicted of tax evasion and sent to prison (1931)
Republic Steel Memorial Day Massacre: police kill ten striking workers outside Republic Steel's South Chicago plant (May 30, 1937)
WPA and CCC employ thousands of Illinoisans in public works projects
Bronzeville's cultural life flourishes despite the Depression — gospel, blues, and literary achievements
Richard Wright publishes 'Native Son' (1940, but written in the late 1930s in Chicago)
1930s.economic_conditions: The Great Depression hit Chicago and Illinois with devastating force. Meatpacking, steel, and manufacturing laid off workers en masse. Unemployment in Chicago reached catastrophic levels — some neighborhoods exceeded 50%. Downstate coal mining and agriculture were equally devastated. New Deal programs — WPA, CCC, PWA — provided essential relief, employing thousands in public works. The Century of Progress Exposition attracted millions and provided a psychological boost. The steel industry's labor conflicts — culminating in the Memorial Day Massacre — defined the decade's class warfare. Recovery was slow and incomplete.
1930s.migration_patterns: The Depression slowed but did not stop the Great Migration — conditions in the South remained worse than in Chicago. Some unemployed workers returned to family farms downstate. Mexican workers faced deportation campaigns — thousands were repatriated from Chicago. Internal migration within Illinois continued as people sought any available work. Young men joined the CCC. The Black Belt's boundaries remained rigidly enforced by racial covenants and violence despite overcrowding.
1930s.social_context: The Depression radicalized Chicago's working class. The CIO's organizing drives in steel, meatpacking, and other industries created a powerful labor movement. The Memorial Day Massacre of 1937 — police shooting unarmed strikers — was one of the decade's most shocking events. Bronzeville's cultural life was remarkable despite poverty — gospel music (Thomas Dorsey, Mahalia Jackson), blues (Tampa Red, Big Bill Broonzy), literature (Richard Wright), and journalism (the Chicago Defender) flourished. The Depression hit Black Chicagoans hardest — last hired, first fired. The Democratic political machine under Anton Cermak and later Ed Kelly consolidated power, incorporating Black voters.
1930s.daily_life_snapshot: Depression-era Chicago was a city of breadlines, shuttered factories, and desperate improvisation. Families doubled and tripled up in apartments. Evictions were common — neighbors sometimes moved evicted families' furniture back inside. WPA workers built parks, bridges, and public buildings. Bronzeville's church culture — gospel choirs, Sunday dinners, mutual aid societies — sustained communities. The Century of Progress fair offered a brief, glittering escape. Downstate farm families lost land to foreclosure. Coal miners worked reduced hours or not at all. Radio was free entertainment, and the movies cost a dime.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Great Depression — unemployment reaches 50% in some Chicago neighborhoods
Memorial Day Massacre — police kill ten striking steelworkers (May 30, 1937)
Mass evictions and homelessness in Chicago
Mexican repatriation campaigns (1930s)
Farm foreclosures across downstate Illinois
Coal mine closures in southern Illinois
narrative_hooks: A steelworker and his family march toward the Republic Steel plant on Memorial Day 1937, carrying an American flag — police open fire, killing ten and wounding dozens
Mahalia Jackson sings gospel in a Bronzeville church basement in the 1930s, her voice filling a room of people who have nothing except their faith
A family visits the Century of Progress Exposition in 1933, marveling at a vision of the future while their present is defined by unemployment and uncertainty
Richard Wright sits in a South Side rooming house writing stories about the Black experience in Chicago that will shake American literature

key_events: World War II mobilizes Illinois's industrial capacity — Chicago's factories and stockyards run at full capacity
Enrico Fermi achieves the first controlled nuclear chain reaction at the University of Chicago (December 2, 1942)
Great Migration surges — Chicago's Black population grows from 278,000 (1940) to 492,000 (1950)
Women enter factories in unprecedented numbers — 'Rosie the Riveter' era
Restrictive housing covenants upheld by Illinois courts confine Black Chicagoans to the South Side
Bronzeville's nightlife and cultural scene reaches its zenith — Muddy Waters, blues clubs on 47th Street
GI Bill transforms veterans' lives — college education and suburban homeownership
1940s.economic_conditions: World War II ended the Depression and transformed Illinois into the nation's industrial heartland. Chicago's factories produced everything from steel to radar components. The stockyards supplied meat to the military. War contracts enriched manufacturers. Full employment returned, wages rose, and overtime was abundant. After the war, pent-up consumer demand fueled a sustained boom. The GI Bill created a new middle class. Suburbanization began as veterans bought homes in new developments. Downstate agriculture thrived on wartime demand.
1940s.migration_patterns: The wartime Great Migration brought over 200,000 additional Black Southerners to Chicago. The Black Belt expanded and new Black neighborhoods formed, though restrictive covenants and white resistance limited where Black families could live. Appalachian whites migrated to Chicago for factory jobs, settling in Uptown and other neighborhoods. Mexican migration resumed after wartime labor shortages. After the war, white veterans moved to suburbs, beginning the white flight that would transform Chicago. Black veterans, excluded from suburban housing by discrimination, remained in the city.
1940s.social_context: World War II brought opportunity and injustice in equal measure. Black workers gained factory jobs but faced discrimination. The Double V Campaign demanded victory over fascism and racism. Restrictive covenants, upheld by Illinois courts, created intense overcrowding in Black neighborhoods. Bronzeville reached its cultural peak — Muddy Waters brought Delta blues to Chicago, transforming it into electric blues. The Chicago Defender championed civil rights. After the war, suburban white flight and urban Black concentration created the foundations of the modern racial divide. The University of Chicago's atomic research changed the world.
1940s.daily_life_snapshot: Wartime Chicago was a city mobilized for production and for culture. Factories ran three shifts. Victory gardens, rationing, and scrap drives defined daily life. Bronzeville's nightlife was spectacular — 47th Street clubs drew crowds to hear blues, jazz, and gospel. After the war, suburban development exploded — tract houses, shopping centers, and drive-in theaters appeared on Chicago's periphery. Television arrived. The stockyards still dominated the South Side's smell and economy. Downstate life continued its agricultural rhythms, though returned veterans brought new ambitions and expectations.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: World War II casualties — thousands of Illinoisans killed and wounded
Restrictive housing covenants create severe overcrowding in Black neighborhoods
Wartime rationing and shortages
Airport Homes race riot — white mobs attack Black veterans moving into housing project (1946)
Continued racial violence against Black families seeking better housing
narrative_hooks: Enrico Fermi watches the needle climb on a radiation detector under the University of Chicago's squash court on December 2, 1942 — humanity has split the atom, and nothing will be the same
Muddy Waters plugs in an electric guitar on the South Side in the 1940s and invents a sound that will become rock and roll
A Black veteran returns from liberating Europe to find he cannot rent an apartment outside the overcrowded South Side
A Mexican woman works at a Chicago defense plant during the war, earning her own money for the first time

key_events: Chicago's Black population continues growing — reaches 813,000 by 1960
Emmett Till, a Chicago teenager, murdered in Mississippi (August 28, 1955) — his mother's decision for an open-casket funeral galvanizes the civil rights movement
Richard J. Daley elected mayor (1955) — begins the most powerful political machine in American history
Public housing high-rises (Robert Taylor Homes, Cabrini-Green) built, concentrating Black poverty
Suburbanization accelerates — white flight from Chicago to surrounding suburbs
Interstate Highway System construction transforms the Chicago metropolitan area
Meatpacking industry begins leaving Chicago — Union Stock Yards decline
1950s.economic_conditions: Chicago's economy was at peak strength in some sectors while already declining in others. Meatpacking began leaving the city as the Union Stock Yards became obsolete — decentralized meatpacking closer to feedlots replaced the old centralized model. Steel production in South Chicago and the Gary corridor remained strong. Manufacturing diversified into electronics, chemicals, and consumer goods. The service sector grew. Suburban commercial development began drawing retail and jobs from the city. Downstate agriculture consolidated as small farms gave way to large operations. Coal mining in southern Illinois continued its long decline.
1950s.migration_patterns: White flight from Chicago to suburbs was the defining migration pattern. New suburbs — Park Forest, Skokie, Oak Lawn, Downers Grove — grew explosively. FHA lending practices and restrictive covenants effectively barred Black families from most suburbs. Black migration from the South continued, pushing Chicago's Black population over 800,000 by 1960. Puerto Rican migration to Chicago's North Side neighborhoods grew. Downstate depopulation continued as agricultural consolidation and coal mine closures eliminated rural livelihoods.
1950s.social_context: The murder of Emmett Till in Mississippi in 1955 and his mother Mamie Till-Bradley's decision to hold an open-casket funeral in Chicago was a watershed moment in the civil rights movement. Jet magazine published the photos that horrified the nation. Richard J. Daley's election as mayor in 1955 began a dynasty that would dominate Chicago politics for decades. His machine delivered services to loyal voters — including the Black South Side — while maintaining segregation through public housing policies that concentrated Black poverty in high-rise projects. The Daley machine was simultaneously progressive on some issues and deeply resistant to racial integration.
1950s.daily_life_snapshot: Suburban Chicagoland families lived the 1950s American Dream — ranch houses, backyard barbecues, new schools, and station wagons. Urban families, especially on the South Side and West Side, faced a different reality — overcrowded apartments, deteriorating schools, and limited services. The 'L' trains connected the city, and expressways began cutting through neighborhoods. Television became universal. Rock and roll arrived — Chess Records on the South Side recorded Muddy Waters, Chuck Berry, and Howlin' Wolf. High school basketball and football were community events. Daley's machine kept the streets paved and the garbage collected, asking only for votes in return.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Murder of Emmett Till (August 28, 1955) — his funeral in Chicago galvanizes civil rights movement
Public housing high-rises concentrate Black poverty (Robert Taylor Homes, Cabrini-Green)
Redlining and restrictive covenants enforce housing segregation
Union Stock Yards begin closing — loss of meatpacking jobs
Our Lady of the Angels School fire kills 92 children and 3 nuns in Chicago (December 1, 1958)
narrative_hooks: Mamie Till-Bradley insists on an open casket at her son Emmett's funeral in September 1955 — 'I want the world to see what they did to my boy' — and the photos change history
Richard J. Daley sits in his fifth-floor City Hall office, the most powerful political boss in America, deciding who gets what in a city where loyalty is the only currency that matters
A family in the Robert Taylor Homes looks out from the sixteenth floor at a city that has stacked them up and fenced them in
A parent at Our Lady of the Angels School runs toward the burning building on December 1, 1958, as 92 children and 3 nuns die inside

key_events: Martin Luther King Jr. brings the civil rights movement to Chicago — the Chicago Freedom Movement campaigns against housing segregation (1966)
MLK marches through Marquette Park; white mobs throw rocks and bottles — King says he has 'never seen such hate' (August 5, 1966)
Democratic National Convention in Chicago erupts in violence — police beat antiwar protesters (August 1968)
Chicago's West Side erupts in riots after Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination (April 4–7, 1968)
Union Stock Yards close (1971, but declining through the 1960s)
Fred Hampton, Black Panther leader, assassinated in his bed by Chicago police and FBI (December 4, 1969)
Daley machine maintains iron grip on city politics through patronage and power
1960s.economic_conditions: The 1960s marked the acceleration of Chicago's deindustrialization. The Union Stock Yards were closing. Steel production in South Chicago faced competition. Manufacturing began moving to suburbs, the Sun Belt, and overseas. The service economy grew but many jobs paid less than factory work. Federal programs — War on Poverty, Model Cities — directed resources to struggling neighborhoods but could not reverse the structural changes. Downstate agriculture continued consolidating. Southern Illinois coal country was in chronic depression. Suburban economic growth increasingly came at the expense of the city.
1960s.migration_patterns: White flight from Chicago continued at an accelerating pace — the city lost over 400,000 white residents during the 1960s. Black neighborhoods expanded into formerly white areas on the West Side and South Side, often through blockbusting and panic selling. Puerto Rican and Mexican communities grew in Humboldt Park, Pilsen, and Little Village. Appalachian whites in Uptown organized around community issues. Black middle-class families began leaving the most troubled neighborhoods for better areas within the city. Suburban growth continued explosively.
1960s.social_context: The 1960s were the most turbulent decade in Chicago's history since the 1890s. Martin Luther King Jr.'s Chicago Freedom Movement of 1966 challenged housing segregation and exposed Northern racism to the nation. King's march through Marquette Park met a white mob so violent that King said he had 'never seen such hate, not in Mississippi or Alabama.' The 1968 Democratic Convention's 'police riot' broadcast Chicago's brutality to the world. The assassination of Fred Hampton by police and the FBI was one of the era's most shocking acts of state violence. The Daley machine survived all of it — battered but intact — through patronage, loyalty, and ruthless political skill.
1960s.daily_life_snapshot: Chicago in the 1960s was a city at war with itself. Black neighborhoods on the West Side and South Side suffered from disinvestment, overcrowding, and aggressive policing. White ethnic neighborhoods fiercely resisted integration. Young activists marched, organized, and sometimes rioted. The 1968 convention turned Grant Park into a battlefield. But daily life continued — people worked, raised children, attended church, and tried to improve their neighborhoods. Blues and soul music flourished. Mexican and Puerto Rican communities built vibrant cultural institutions. Downstate Illinois watched Chicago's turmoil from a distance, more certain than ever that the city and the state were different worlds.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: MLK's Marquette Park march met with violent white mobs (August 5, 1966)
West Side riots after MLK's assassination — widespread destruction (April 1968)
Democratic National Convention police riot (August 1968)
Assassination of Fred Hampton by police and FBI (December 4, 1969)
Continued housing segregation and urban poverty
Vietnam War casualties among Illinois service members
Deindustrialization — factory closures and job losses
narrative_hooks: Martin Luther King Jr. ducks as a rock thrown by a white mob in Marquette Park strikes his shoulder on August 5, 1966 — he has marched in Selma and Birmingham, but Chicago's hate shocks even him
A young Black Panther named Fred Hampton falls asleep in his West Side apartment on December 4, 1969 — Chicago police and the FBI will make sure he never wakes up
A delegate at the 1968 Democratic Convention watches from the Hilton as police beat young protesters on Michigan Avenue while the whole world watches on television
A steelworker in South Chicago reads about another plant closing and wonders if the city that Carl Sandburg called 'City of the Big Shoulders' still has room for men like him
